severity_rationale: State parameter is used as origin-tracking payload, NOT as anti-CSRF nonce. Attacker can craft a fully-formed authorize URL with arbitrary state; victim click completes flow on attacker's terms. Classic OAuth login-CSRF. Prior report-v001 mentioned state nonce absence but did not isolate this as a separate finding — report as NEW primary CSRF finding.
validation_method: Curl — 4 different attacker-controlled state JSON payloads all echoed unchanged through hops 0-2.
response_summary: state is used purely as an origin-tracking payload, not as an anti-CSRF nonce. There is no server-side mapping of state to the initiator's session; any value is accepted and echoed back at callback.
impact: OAuth login-CSRF: attacker forces victim to complete an OAuth round-trip on attacker's chosen terms (e.g., linking victim's 23andMe auth to attacker's downstream account, or forcing a specific origin_uri). Combined with F-A-004's account-identifier leak, attacker can observe victim's account correlator on every triggered flow.
chain: Core link in F-A-001 + F-A-003 + F-A-004 OAuth-CSRF + info-disclosure chain.
remediation: Generate cryptographically random state nonce, bind server-side to initiator session, verify on callback. Keep origin_uri in a separate server-side-stored field, not in state.

reportability_audit.prior_report_similarity: 0.4 — partial overlap with v001 (mentioned state as origin tracker), but v001 framed it as 'no CSRF nonce' under a larger chain. This isolates the CSRF-nonce gap as a standalone. Keep.
